about: The practice was applied to a new 380kV transmission line in Schleswig-Holstein, Northern Germany, over three years (2012–2014) of developing, implementing, and evaluating the dialogue process. The procedure and insights gained can be leveraged for any grid expansion project throughout Germany and Europe. Schleswig-Holstein has the best wind power conditions in Germany due to its coastal regions. With increasing shares of renewable energy generation, grid expansion projects are essential to transport generated power to consumption centres in South and West Germany. Successful cooperation between the state, local governments, and the project developer can greatly accelerate grid expansion projects. To support this, project partners agreed on a realisation agreement forming the foundation for the dialogue process. Its core ideas were to implement an early, informal dialogue in the region with regional conferences, specialist and citizen dialogues, and to establish a clear, transparent, and ambitious schedule for planning, approval, and construction. The dialogue was divided into a citizens’ dialogue, moderated by the environmental NGO German Environmental Aid (DUH), a planning dialogue, and a plan approval procedure. Outcomes of these dialogues influenced the project across multiple areas. Technically, 80% of the new 380kV line was designed to be strung on the same pylons as an existing 110kV line. In a regional bird migration area, the old 110kV line will be removed and replaced by a ground cable to avoid overlapping lines on migration routes. For communities, the largest possible distances to residential buildings were planned, and rules were developed for agriculture and nature preservation in affected areas. The realisation agreement and dialogue procedure were subsequently applied to another grid expansion project along the East coast of Schleswig-Holstein (2014–2016).

title: Communication and Participation Concept
desc: Amprion developed a comprehensive communication approach for the routing of the interconnector project “ALEGrO” linking Belgium and Germany, aiming to inform and involve the public early, gather valuable feedback, adapt routing accordingly, and increase acceptance of the interconnector route.
highlights: Implemented an early and comprehensive public communication strategy for the ALEGrO interconnector; Provided an online participation platform allowing the public to give input on routing; Adapted the interconnector route based on feedback to increase local acceptance.
about: The Aachen Liège Electricity Grid Overlay (ALEGrO) is an HVDC underground cable link with a bidirectional rated power of 1,000 MW, and represents the first interconnection between Belgium and Germany. Its objective is to enhance market integration by enabling direct power exchanges between the two countries. Given the project’s importance, Amprion implemented a proactive and interactive information strategy and participation concept before and during the formal planning approval procedure. The public, communities, and associations were informed early and comprehensively about the project, receiving regular background information through various channels. They were informed of proposed routes in a timely manner, enabling them to provide feedback. Amprion provided an online participation platform where the public could comment on the overall project or routing specifics. The information received was used to adapt the cable routing accordingly, thus increasing acceptance of the infrastructure.

title: Early involvement of landowners
desc: Danish TSO Energinet applied a communication methodology to involve landowners affected by a 150 kV cable project in the early planning stages – before and during the authority permitting procedures. The approach aimed to integrate local knowledge, increase transparency, and give landowners the possibility to influence the project.
highlights: Implemented local knowledge into the planning of the project; Increased transparency and understanding of the project locally; Gave landowners the opportunity to influence the project through active participation; Increased overall acceptance of the project; Reduced errors and setbacks due to public and landowner protests.
about: Energinet developed a communication approach to better involve both local neighbourhoods and directly affected landowners at the early stages of a new grid project. During the official corridor planning stage, Energinet held meetings in local neighbourhoods with relevant landowners invited. Instead of organising individual meetings with each landowner once the corridor was fixed, the company discussed the cable route with groups of neighbours, as changes often affected entire districts. The project Han Herred–Thy–Mors–Salling aims to maintain and expand the transmission grid in North-Western Jutland to integrate more wind power into the Danish power system. Several neighbourhood meetings provided valuable inputs that influenced the final corridor for the cable route, including advice on special farming operations (e.g. Christmas tree cultivation), distinct soil conditions, and drainage challenges. Energinet also organised four targeted neighbourhood dialogue meetings with landowners. In these group sessions, participants received a map and a string and were asked to reach an agreement on the exact cable route collectively. Feedback from landowners was very positive, with evaluations showing that the approach increased involvement, improved shared knowledge, and reduced conflicts. Energinet is the Danish TSO, a non-profit enterprise owned by the Ministry of Energy, Utilities and Climate. It owns the national electricity and gas transmission systems and is responsible for the security of supply and the development of grid infrastructure. The company also cooperated with researchers from the University of Aalborg on stakeholder and citizen involvement.

title: Educational Material on History of Electricity
desc: Litgrid, the Lithuanian transmission operator, created an educational website to explain the history of electricity in Lithuania and the functioning of the national grid to the general public. The website increases understanding of electricity production, delivery, and grid management, inspiring interest in electrical engineering.
highlights: The website was created for the 70th anniversary of the Lithuanian power system; Interactive elements include games and visuals to engage users; Covers generation, transmission, distribution, and the role of the transmission system operator.
about: The electricity sector is complex and difficult to understand for those not directly involved. For this reason, Litgrid developed an educational website showcasing the history of electricity in Lithuania. The website explains how electricity is produced and delivered to consumers, how power flows are managed, and the role of the transmission system operator. The story uses clear examples, expressive visuals, and simple games, encouraging user participation through interactivity. This practice provides a comprehensive overview of the country’s electric system, including power generation from both traditional resources and renewable energy plants, transmission and distribution networks, and consumers. Community engagement is crucial in grid development projects, and the website aims to increase public awareness of the power system, highlighting why it is relevant and how it functions in detail. Litgrid AB is the Lithuanian electricity transmission system operator. It maintains stable national power system operations, controls electricity flows, and supports competition in the domestic electricity market. Litgrid integrates the national power system into the European infrastructure and electricity market and has implemented two strategic cross-border links: NordBalt (Lithuania-Sweden) and LitPol Link (Lithuania-Poland).

title: EirGrid Community Support Fund
desc: EirGrid developed a Community Support Fund in order to provide compensation to local communities who are located closest to new transmission infrastructure. The fund recognises the importance of local communities and voluntary organisations aiming to address issues that those particular communities are facing and provides them with compensation in the form of grants.
highlights: In 2016, 37 community groups received funding from the first Community Fund – The Mullingar-Kinnegad fund (music, athletic and sporting organisations, childcare services and support for senior citizens); Working collaboratively with independent organisations to decide who benefits from the Community Fund; This initiative plays an important part in contributing to increased public acceptance of grid projects
about: The practice is applied to a specific geographical area surrounding any newly built transmission line. The fund is calculated based on the length and voltage of the new transmission line. For example, in the case of the Mullingar–Kinnegad 110 kV line, a €360,000 fund was made available to organisations within 2 km on either side of the 34 km line. When the electricity grid is expanded, communities located close to newly built infrastructure may be affected. Recognising the importance of supporting these local communities is vital for a project’s successful implementation. In January 2014, EirGrid developed the Community Fund initiative following a consultation process where EirGrid listened to public feedback. Through the Community Fund, EirGrid delivers long-lasting benefits to affected communities by granting financial support to organisations that address local issues such as employment, education, improvement of local facilities, and energy efficiency initiatives. The benefits of this approach have already been demonstrated. Feedback from communities has helped improve the practice, while the initiative has also encouraged a shift within EirGrid towards acknowledging the importance of building trust and establishing open, transparent communication as key to public acceptance of grid infrastructure projects. EirGrid, the Irish Transmission System Operator (TSO), developed the practice over two years in cooperation with the Community Foundation of Ireland (CFI) and in close communication with the Irish Department of Communications, Climate Action and Environment, the Irish Energy Regulator, and representative bodies. The Community Fund has since become part of EirGrid’s company policy and will be made available for any new transmission infrastructure project.

title: ID4AL Project: Hierarchical and distributed automation for medium voltage (MV) and low voltage (LV) grids
desc: The EU-funded IDE4L project demonstrated a system of distribution network automation, IT systems, and functions enabling active network management. The distributed decision-making architecture improves real-time monitoring and controllability of MV and LV grids, efficiently integrating renewable energy and new loads without compromising reliability.
highlights: 6 laboratory demonstrations and 3 field demonstrations validating the developed architecture, monitoring, control and protection systems in real physical grids with connections to PV panels, wind turbines, heat pumps and actual consumers; Decentralised Fault Location Isolation and Supply Restoration (FLISR) solution based on IEC 61850 GOOSE including adaptation to grid topology or DER configuration changes is ready for market launch; 3-4 times increase of grid capacity compared to existing passive grid reinforcement methods; Interoperability through standard-based interfaces and scalability of automation system; Reduced reaction time to varying conditions and workload for control centre operators
about: The emergence of distributed renewable energy resources has increased the complexity of managing MV and LV grids. Challenges include exploiting existing networks efficiently, maintaining reliability and power quality, reducing demand uncertainties, and resolving conflicts between market needs and technical constraints. IDE4L was conceptualised to address these challenges by designing advanced automation systems that utilise ancillary services of distributed energy resources and aggregators. Key functionalities include decentralised FLISR, real-time monitoring, state estimation, and congestion management. The system manages fast charging conditions and optimises use of the existing distribution network by exchanging dynamic information and services among flexibility market actors, including DSOs, commercial aggregators, and TSOs. The Tampere University of Technology coordinated the EU FP7-funded IDE4L project (2013-16), which involved research institutes, university faculties, associations, technical solution providers, DSOs, and energy providers from six European countries.

title: MARES Real Time Automatic Control for PSH in Small Isolated Systems
desc: Red Eléctrica de España (REE)’s real-time monitoring tool MARES enables safe, high-level renewable energy integration on the island of El Hierro. Designed for isolated grids, MARES helps balance wind energy and pumped hydro storage, reducing diesel dependency and ensuring grid stability.
highlights: MARES helped El Hierro reach 40 hours of 100% renewable electricity in 2016; In 2019, renewables covered 54% of the island’s electricity demand; The tool minimises blackout risks by automating real-time energy management in an isolated grid.
about: The island of El Hierro relies on a hybrid energy system combining wind power and a pumped hydro storage facility. Wind energy is used when available; when there is surplus, water is pumped uphill for later electricity generation. MARES, REE’s “Real-Time Automatic Management” tool, monitors and manages this process with automation algorithms, allowing fast-response decisions that keep the system stable and resilient. Before MARES, only 2% of El Hierro’s electricity came from renewables. After implementation, average renewable penetration exceeded 20%, peaking at 50%. In one milestone, the island ran for 40 continuous hours on 100% renewable energy. MARES has optimised operators’ tasks, reduced system costs, cut diesel use, and enhanced environmental outcomes. The power system served as the testbed for the tool. Since deployment, no major disturbances have occurred. Dispatchers continue to monitor its performance and suggest improvements. REE — Spain’s electricity transmission operator — developed MARES as part of its mission to ensure secure, sustainable energy for its 45 million users.

title: Objective Osprey
desc: Launched in 2016 during RTE’s Nature Festival, the Objective Osprey project brings together ONF, Loiret Nature Environnement, RTE and the City of Orléans. It aims to preserve and improve knowledge of the osprey, a rare and vulnerable bird of prey in France, through cameras, observatories, research and public awareness.
highlights: The Ravoir observatory, built in 1996, is the only French site where nesting ospreys can be observed without disturbance; The project uses cameras on pylons and in natural habitats to monitor osprey behaviour, diet, and reproduction while sharing live images with the public; Objective Osprey was runner-up for Environmental Protection in RGI’s Good Practice of the Year Award 2016.
about: The Objective Osprey project was created to preserve and deepen knowledge of the osprey, an emblematic bird of prey in the Centre Val de Loire region. Cameras installed at nests, including historical cameras set up by LNE in 2000 and new ones on RTE pylons in Sologne in 2016, provide valuable information without disturbing the species. These images allow observation of behaviour, diet and predation, and are shared with the public to raise awareness. The project is based on three pillars. Regional anchoring connects the initiative to the osprey’s return to the Orléans forest in the 1980s, which has since grown into France’s largest osprey population. Research programs at RTE and MOBE study diet and the role of video monitoring in species preservation, with results published in scientific journals. Educational support includes visits to the Ravoir observatory, a live-stream website, and active social media channels. The Ravoir observatory, built by ONF in 1996, is located at the Ravoir pond in Orléans forest. It is open from April to August, allowing the public to observe a nesting pair of ospreys without disturbance. LNE staff welcome visitors every Sunday. In 2021, more than 20 nests were recorded on pylons across France. In the Loiret, around 25 pairs breed annually, with 50 at the regional level and 117 nationally. The population is expanding slowly but steadily. RTE continues to manage the Facebook and Instagram pages of Objectif Balbuzard, providing updates to more than 1,300 followers. The project combines conservation, research, education, and communication to protect this rare species.

title: PAS System for Bird-Friendly Grid
desc: EVN Bulgaria implemented bird-friendly power lines by insulating grid components and adding protective elements to overhead lines. The practice, part of the EU-funded Life for Safe Grid project, prevents electrocution and collisions of the Imperial Eagle and other bird species, while also enhancing grid security and reducing electricity supply disruptions.
highlights: The PAS system combines insulation, protective fittings, perches, and wing space diverters in one holistic bird-friendly design; The first implementation was along a 15 km line crossing the Sakar Special Protected Area, home to 10 breeding pairs of Imperial Eagles; Over 78 percent of juvenile Imperial Eagles in Bulgaria died from electrocution between 2009 and 2012, making this solution vital.
about: The insulation of power lines is a common practice for bird protection. The PAS system is innovative as it combines various protective measures in one efficient and bird-friendly system. It consists of insulated wires with weatherproof synthetic VPE coating, fittings and connectors, consoles, perches providing higher and safer resting places for birds, and wing space diverters ensuring a safe gap between poles and perching areas. The first implementation took place along a 15 km stretch of the “Balgarin” overhead power line. This line crosses the Sakar Special Protected Area, part of the Natura 2000 network and the most important SPA for the Imperial Eagle in Bulgaria. The area supports 10 breeding pairs and is critical for young birds during their dispersion period. The Imperial Eagle is classed as vulnerable on the Red List of Threatened Species. Aboveground electric infrastructure has been proven to be the major threat to juvenile and immature eagles in Bulgaria, with over 78 percent of juvenile deaths caused by electrocution between 2009 and 2012. Evaluation of the PAS system’s effect on bird mortality was planned for 2018, but early results indicated reduced disturbances on the grid since its installation, a strong sign of success for both bird protection and electricity supply reliability. EVN Bulgaria Elektrorazpredelenie, the electricity distribution company responsible for the project, operates across South-East Bulgaria, supplying over 1.5 million customers in an area of nearly 42,000 square kilometres. The practice was carried out within the Life for Safe Grid project funded by the European Commission’s Life programme, in cooperation with the Bulgarian Society for the Protection of Birds. It covers eight protected areas within the Natura 2000 network in Bulgaria.

title: Promotion of biogas systems in rural Kenya
desc: The Namalere Forest Conservation Group (NFCYG) promotes off-grid solutions combined with biogas as an alternative source of energy in Kenya to improve social welfare. This includes better living conditions and health for inhabitants, environmental protection, and sustainability through an efficient and cost-saving approach.
highlights: Installed two large-scale and 150 small-scale biogas generation projects at schools, markets, and homesteads, plus a 1.8 km long-distance heating off-grid network, giving at least 500 homesteads access to clean, affordable energy; Biogas plants use locally available raw materials such as manure, human, and agricultural waste, reducing waste, greenhouse gas emissions, and energy costs; Improved indoor air quality by reducing respiratory and eye diseases caused by traditional cooking fires and lighting; Reduced the workload for women and children related to fuel wood collection and cooking activities; Provided technical training for women to maintain biogas plants, increasing employment and income opportunities; Plans to develop a modular biogas system, with a target of installing 2,000 digesters in the area before expanding, potentially benefiting 1.9 million people and creating around 300 jobs.
about: The NFCYG is a Kenyan NGO dedicated to energy conservation and reducing deforestation in western Kenya. It supports community-driven efforts to address environmental threats and develops biogas as an alternative energy source for institutions and households. In addition to environmental goals, the practice pursues social objectives, such as creating employment, empowering women and youth, and promoting sustainable use of forest resources.

title: Public Consultation - Your Grid, Your Views, Your Tomorrow
desc: EirGrid launched a nationwide public consultation, Your Grid, Your Views, Your Tomorrow, to involve citizens in shaping Ireland’s grid development strategy. The initiative, welcomed by the Irish government, marked the first time the public played a central role in grid planning.
highlights: Over 60,000 people reached through consultation; 3,386 formal responses received; first national initiative with direct citizen input in grid planning; wide use of digital and offline communication tools; plain English report summary for accessibility.
about: EirGrid carried out a comprehensive national public consultation entitled Your Grid, Your Views, Your Tomorrow to define Ireland’s future grid development strategy. This was the first large-scale initiative to emphasize the role of citizens in planning, aiming to enhance public awareness, foster dialogue, and build trust in the transmission system development process. The engagement approach combined multiple communication channels and tools to reach citizens and stakeholders. EirGrid connected with over 60,000 people through a dedicated website, social media, a bespoke online portal, advertising campaigns, marketing communications, regional forums (also live streamed), stakeholder and one-to-one briefings, and a nationwide mail drop. In total, 3,386 responses were received, providing valuable feedback for shaping the grid strategy. The consultation outcomes were published in September 2015 in the report Your Grid, Your Views, Your Tomorrow. This document reflected both the contributions of the public and an updated economic perspective for grid development. A plain English summary was also produced to ensure the information was clear and accessible to a non-technical audience. Work on the final grid development strategy remains ongoing. EirGrid, a state-owned company, has operated and developed Ireland’s high-voltage electricity grid since 2006. It ensures a secure and reliable supply of electricity, operating power flows and planning future grid needs. This practice was developed in collaboration with a wide range of stakeholders, including the Department of Communications, Energy & Natural Resources, the Department of Jobs, Enterprise & Innovation, elected representatives, IDA Ireland, regional interest groups, local communities, and the general public.

title: Public Consultation Your Grid, Your Views, Your Tomorrow
desc: EirGrid carried out a national public consultation on its strategy to develop Ireland’s grid in the future. Entitled “Your Grid, Your Views, Your Tomorrow”, this engagement initiative was the first of its kind, with an emphasis on the citizen’s role in how Ireland’s grid is developed. The initiative was welcomed by the Irish government. Objectives 01 Enhancing public […]

title: PV energy data service
desc: SMA Solar Technology AG uses near-time data from small and medium-sized photovoltaic (PV) systems in the TenneT grid area to create precise, geographically detailed PV generation extrapolations and forecasts. This practice helps make solar energy more predictable, reducing the need for expensive control reserves and supporting grid reliability.
highlights: Reduces PV extrapolation delay from 40 to 10 minutes using near-time data; Uses data from 20,000 PV systems (300 MW) out of 660,000 (15 GW total in TenneT area); Supports congestion management and reduces grid fees through accurate forecasting.
about: The variability of photovoltaic (PV) production can cause imbalances in the power system, which must be compensated for using costly control power. In Germany’s TenneT control area, with over 660,000 PV systems installed (15 GW total), SMA Solar Technology AG uses data from 20,000 of these systems (300 MW) to improve forecast accuracy. By accessing data through online PV monitoring portals, SMA creates highly resolved PV generation extrapolations and forecasts. Thanks to the use of near-time values, the delay in extrapolations has been reduced from 40 to just 10 minutes. This leads to better integration of solar power into the grid, increased system stability, reduced need for balancing and congestion management, and ultimately lower grid fees. The forecasts also support electricity marketing, control reserve reduction, feed-in management validation, and planning, thereby improving overall social welfare. SMA is a global leader in PV system technology with operations in 20 countries and over 3,000 employees. TenneT, the transmission system operator covering parts of Germany and the Netherlands, manages a high-voltage grid spanning 21,000 km and serves over 41 million end-users.

title: Real-time technology for the effective integration of distributed energy resources
desc: Alpiq developed a real-time data-to-decision platform that offers products and services to its customers and partners for all aspects around energy and flexibility markets. The self-learning algorithms deployed in the cloud platform catalyse the effective integration of Distributed Energy Resources (DER) through the optimal dispatch of flexible generation and load.
highlights: Making use of synergies between different types of assets throughout the whole energy value chain and enable their easy and efficient dispatch to short-term markets; Self-learning algorithms (fundamental DER model, self-learning forecasts, stochastic optimisation) enabling full automation of data-to-decisions processes; Rapid modification of algorithms to fit changing regulatory and market incentives; Scalable and modular framework based on micro-services, combining different competences of partners’ assets and solutions
about: The international energy markets are becoming increasingly complex, integrated, and distributed. Effective DER utilisation requires a flexible framework that accommodates uncertainties and enables rapid, autonomous adaptation and holistic integration. Alpiq’s Digital Technology & Innovations team developed an integrated platform linking all energy stakeholders’ assets for optimal management. All types of systems can be connected to the platform, including smart homes, electric car charging networks, large industrial consumers, centralised and decentralised power stations, solar systems, storage facilities, and electricity grids. The platform processes all available data in real-time, calculates optimal decision options, and can dispatch them directly. Applications include peak-shaving, demand response, and aggregation of capacities for Virtual Power Plants, enabling partners to generate additional revenues. Alpiq demonstrated a commercial showcase on a waste water treatment plant, optimising two flexible sewage-gas powered generators to reduce grid usage during periods of high tariffs. Alpiq is a leading energy company in Switzerland aiming to become Europe’s top smart energy services provider. The Digital Technology & Innovation unit focuses on developing self-learning algorithms to connect and optimise flexible and decentralised assets across the energy sector.

title: Recovery of Posidonia Oceanica seagrass meadows
highlights: After one year of planting, the survival rate was over 60%; 51% of the fragments planted in 2016 had generated new shoots after 6 months, 18% of the fragments had generated new shoots after 1 year; A review in 2020 showed a confirmed survival rate of >90%; REE has published a methodological guide on developing an open sea grass meadow recovery method including technical, material and economic viability; A separate research project from 2020-21 also showed that Posidonia has considerable positive effects for combatting ocean plastic, as it filters these and fuses them with natural fibre bundles called “Neptune Balls”; In 2021, REE announced that they would, together with the Regional Ministry of the Environment and Territory of the Balearic Islands and the Mediterranean Institute for Advanced Studies (IMEDEA), carry out a four-year long planned control and monitoring programme; In 2021, a virtual exhibition was launched, which is available on REE’s website in Spanish and allows interested readers to learn much more about the project and the value of Posidonia Oceanica
button_url: Read more here, including explanatory video and technical guide: https://www.ree.es

title: Tests to prove reliability of large-scale solar PV
org: California ISO, First Solar, National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL)
desc: California ISO - CAISO, the US National Renewable Energy Laboratory - NREL and solar PV solutions company, First Solar demonstrated how large-scale solar resources can provide essential reliability services to the grid, which have traditionally been offered by conventional power plants. A series of technical tests conducted on a 300 MW PV plant showcased the role of advanced controls in leveraging the solar energy’s value and contributing to grid stability.
highlights: Most extensive testing and demonstration of reliability services involving PV generation ever conducted in the world; Reducing the need for conventional power plants to provide reliability services; More accurate ramping of PV plant’s power output compared to conventional power plants; Cost savings through eliminating the need for voltage control devices after sunset; Smart inverter technology combined with advanced plant controls allowing multiple PV resources to operate as a single resource
about: California utilities are required by 2030 to produce 50% of electricity sales from renewable energy sources. To achieve this, grid operators need additional methods to maintain reliability and balance generation with variable renewable generation. CAISO, in collaboration with NREL and First Solar, conducted tests demonstrating the PV plant’s ability to provide upwards/downwards ramping, frequency response, and voltage control. Some PV plants can already provide these reliability services, meaning the practice can be implemented once regulations allow renewable resources to be compensated comparably to conventional plants. The approach can be applied to any large-scale PV plant globally or to clustered PV resources with appropriate power aggregation, communications, and controls. Storage can also be incorporated to enhance system flexibility. Partners involved include CAISO (California’s grid operator), NREL (leading U.S. federal research centre on renewable energies), and First Solar (vertically integrated large-scale PV utility). CAISO plans broad stakeholder outreach to identify barriers for PV resources providing reliability services.

title: Underground high voltage cables (UGCs) – Environmental research and on-site development of innovative solutions
desc: Amprion has been conducting a long-term ecological research programme in rural areas, consisting of 20 years of field experiments, and of validation of experimental findings through monitoring of an UGC project in Germany. Through this project, Amprion aims to increase the understanding of UGCs’ thermal and hydrological impact on the soil, and of any resulting […]

title: Underground high voltage cables (UGCs) – Environmental research and on‑site development of innovative solutions
desc: Amprion has been conducting a long-term ecological research programme in rural areas, consisting of 20 years of field experiments, and of validation of experimental findings through monitoring of an UGC project in Germany. Through this project, Amprion aims to increase the understanding of UGCs’ thermal and hydrological impact on the soil, and of any resulting ecological impacts on flora and fauna. The practice’s findings will offer insights on how to predict and reduce impacts of construction and operation during the project planning phase.
highlights: Field experiment findings show a low (or negligible) ecological impact on agricultural production and soil properties; Amprion carried out an ecological assessment using real data from field experiments, rather than from a theoretical model; The practice aims to reduce UGC impact on soil and farmland, accelerate permitting processes and foster acceptance of UGCs through enhanced learning and understanding, especially with farmers and local stakeholders
about: Underground cables with high voltage ratings have not been widely used in rural areas in Europe. Due to the lack of knowledge with regard to the ecological impact of UGCs, it is not possible to forecast potential negative impacts and address them accordingly during a project’s planning phase. Amprion’s comprehensive long-term research programme spanning over 20 years aims to address this knowledge deficit. Starting in 2005 with a 5-year field experiment of artificial soil heating, the thermal effects for different operating scenarios and hydrological conditions were simulated and recorded. Following this, another 5-year experiment was carried out on a 110kV UGC system operating under extreme loads and different bedding materials. Growth and yield were monitored during cultivation experiments in order to establish the effects on agricultural crops. In 2015, the final stage of the practice started; a 10-year monitoring programme of the first 380kV AC (3.6GW), 3.4km UGC project in Germany (Raesfeld), where an instrument station provides continuous monitoring of the soil’s properties. Amprion, a German TSO operating the high voltage grid from Lower Saxony down to the Alps, carried out the practice in cooperation with the Albert-Ludwigs University of Freiburg (Chair of Soil Ecology) and ERM (environmental consultancy). Other stakeholders that were part of the Steering Committee included the University of Südwestfalen, the Westphalia-Lippe Agricultural Association, the Chamber of Agriculture NRW, and several experts and farmers.

title: Virtual Power Plant Next Pool
desc: Next Kraftwerke developed a Virtual Power Plant (VPP) that uses cloud-computing technology to integrate the flexibility of both power producers and consumers, particularly decentralised renewable energy plants. By aggregating their output, the VPP can provide control reserve to balance fluctuations in the electricity grid.
highlights: Aggregation of over 2,900 medium- and small-scale power-producing and consuming units (1.9 GW).; Integration of assets via the “Next Box” remote-control unit for automated monitoring and control.; Intelligent shifting of production to high-price periods and consumption to low-price periods.; Significant participation from biogas plants, offering steady generation for grid stability.; Provision of secondary and tertiary control reserve to support grid balance.
about: Germany’s energy transition relies heavily on decentralised renewable generation, much of which is variable. This volatility, combined with fluctuating industrial power demand, creates challenges for grid stability and congestion management. Next Kraftwerke’s Virtual Power Plant addresses this by pooling the flexibility of thousands of decentralised units, enabling them to act as a single, controllable entity. The system uses the “Next Box” remote-control technology to monitor and manage each unit, allowing for rapid, highly granular responses to control reserve calls. The VPP optimises operations by scheduling generation during times of higher electricity prices and shifting consumption to lower price periods. With biogas plants forming the largest group of participants due to their steady output, the VPP plays a crucial role in providing secondary and tertiary control reserves for the German grid. Next Kraftwerke is also a certified power trader on European energy exchanges such as EPEX and EEX. While its technology is adaptable to other countries, market-specific regulatory and design differences must be considered for replication.

title: VVMplus Research Project
org: The Danish Centre for Environmental Assessment at Aalborg University
desc: The project aims to increase social acceptance of renewable energy projects through improvements in the Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) process by enabling dialogue and including communities in the process.
highlights: The mapping of current EIA practices; Analysis of current conflicts over renewable energy projects and analysis of the barriers and possibilities of working with social impacts in EIAs; International literature review on the topic; Development of pilot projects; Recommendations for EIA practitioners both in regard to renewable energy projects and projects with similar conflicts, such as new transmission power lines
about: This project aims at improving practices to increase the local acceptance of renewable energy projects, based on the Danish experience that the transition to renewable energy is increasingly challenged by local opposition to e.g. wind turbines and biogas plants. It focuses on Environmental Impact Assessments (EIA), which is a central arena for dialogue and conflicts concerning renewable energy projects, and thus a valuable tool to improve. The project is based on understanding that social impacts are central to the conflicts, but so far have not played a very significant role in EIA reports and processes. The project seeks to understand these issues and propose recommendations for an improved EIA practice. Project partners include: The Department of Wind Energy at The Technical University of Denmark and the Nordic Folkecenter for Renewable Energy.

title: Animated videos for grid expansion
desc: The Bundesnetzagentur developed animated online videos to provide the public with accessible, comprehensive, and reliable information about the five steps in Germany’s complex legislative procedure for grid expansion.
highlights: Five three-minute animated videos explaining each step of the grid expansion procedure.; Uses everyday settings to create personal associations and encourage participation.; Targets younger audiences via YouTube and social media.<, Connects renewable integration with the need for grid expansion.; High engagement compared to other governmental videos.; Inspired ideas for shorter videos on topics like underground cables and AC/DC technology.
about: The project communicates that the energy transition (Energiewende) concerns everyone and highlights opportunities for public participation in grid planning. By providing accurate and comprehensive explanations of the formal procedure, the Bundesnetzagentur aims to increase transparency and reach a wider audience, particularly younger people. Five three-minute animated videos were commissioned, each set in an everyday scenario to foster personal connection and make viewers feel part of the Energiewende process. The videos explain the five main steps in grid planning: Drafting a scenario framework by TSOs, outlining likely developments in the energy landscape.; Constructing a network development plan and assessing its environmental impact. ;Presenting the Federal Requirements Plan to the legislative body.; Conducting federal sectoral planning.; Carrying out the route planning procedure. Three videos have been published so far and shown on YouTube, at conferences, and in public dialogues. Their higher-than-average engagement rates have encouraged the creation of additional short videos on specific grid expansion topics, such as underground cables and AC/DC technology. Audience feedback via YouTube comments and social media is used to evaluate public acceptance and project impact.

title: Application éCO2mix
desc: éCO2mix is a public online application developed by RTE to provide transparent, real-time electricity data in France. It helps users of all backgrounds understand the power system, track renewable integration, and monitor consumption, production, and emissions.
highlights: Over 2.5 million consultations annually.; Nearly 100% availability and almost 5/5 user ratings.; Tracks production, consumption, CO₂ emissions, and power exchanges in real time.
about: éCO2mix is an online application designed by RTE to ensure accessible, high-quality, real-time electricity data for all users. Launched in 2010, it visualises the characteristics of the French power system, making it easier to understand the energy transition and renewable energy integration. Users can monitor national or regional electricity production and consumption, with breakdowns by production mode (hydropower, nuclear, gas, wind, etc.), CO₂ emissions, power exchanges, spot prices, and coverage rates for wind and solar. Monthly analyses and key indicators, such as maximum consumption levels, are also available. The free tool is accessible via web browsers or mobile apps for Android and iOS, which can be downloaded using a QR code. It is widely used by media, policymakers, RTE staff, and stakeholders as a trusted source of energy data. éCO2mix has been upgraded annually, adding functionalities based on user and stakeholder feedback. It receives around 2.5 million consultations and 14 million file downloads per year, with an availability rate near 100% and user ratings close to 5/5. RTE evaluates performance through user statistics and service quality indicators to continually enhance the application. RTE is the French electricity transmission network operator, responsible for operating, maintaining, and developing the high and extra-high voltage network, while ensuring its security and reliability.

title: BALANCE – Power grid game application
desc: Statnett developed a gaming app for mobile devices dealing with the topic of grid expansion in order to increase knowledge of and interest in energy, electricity, the power system, the green electric future and related topics among the general public and stakeholders.
highlights: Increases knowledge about the power system by utilising a simplified grid structure, presented in a landscape resembling SimCity, the whole set up based on a power simulator and Ohm's law, to make challenges realistic; Opens people’s minds to issues and challenges that many have never thought of before; Top-ten game in 35 countries, more than 3 million downloads worldwide
about: BALANCE was launched in 2015 by Statnett and quickly became a success, with an English version introduced in 2016. It was designed for long-term use, with continuous updates such as the option for players to design their own levels. The game addresses the fact that for many people the power grid system is not an area of interest, even though stakeholder knowledge strongly influences how Statnett’s services are perceived. BALANCE is part of Statnett’s broader effort to make the power system engaging, accessible, and relevant, while fostering positive expectations for an electric future. BALANCE was designed to be playful, simple, and entertaining, while still conveying a clear and educational message about how the power system works. Players are challenged to secure electricity supply for communities and cities by avoiding blackouts, keeping the grid functional, and ensuring that the right amount of power is produced at all times to maintain balance. The game proved highly popular, becoming the second most downloaded game in Norway in Winter 2015. Since the release of the English version, BALANCE has been downloaded more than 3 million times worldwide, with daily downloads growing by 10,000–15,000. Statnett’s first round of feedback confirmed that the game meets its objectives: trust in Statnett is 19 percentage points higher among BALANCE players compared to the general public, and perceived knowledge about Statnett and the power system is 23 percentage points higher. BALANCE was developed from scratch by Statnett in collaboration with the agency Hyper Games, with continuous feedback from Tech Centres across Norway.

title: Birds and electricity transmission lines: mapping of flight paths
desc: Red Eléctrica de España (REE) is developing a GIS-based tool to integrate bird flight path data across Spain. This enables planning and construction of power lines with minimal environmental impact and prioritises mitigation actions on existing lines to protect endangered species.
highlights: Covers 45 bird species listed in the Birds Directive and Spanish Catalogue of Endangered Species.;Data shared with all 17 Spanish autonomous communities.;Enhances transparency in environmental assessments and public consultations.;Supports prevention of collisions with high-voltage lines.;Common benchmark for biodiversity conservation in infrastructure projects.
about: The GIS tool combines geographic and environmental information on the presence, routes, and flight paths of endangered birds potentially affected by power line collisions. It focuses on 45 species under the Birds Directive and Spanish Catalogue of Endangered Species. By integrating this data, REE can design projects with the least environmental impact, while also identifying priorities for preventive and corrective measures on existing infrastructure. The tool’s outputs are shared with biodiversity authorities across Spain’s 17 autonomous communities, as well as research entities and environmental organisations. This collaborative approach creates a unified framework for environmental assessments, supports transparency in permitting processes, and strengthens stakeholder engagement. Red Eléctrica de España is the sole transmission agent and operator of the Spanish electricity system, connecting 45 million people and employing 1,700 staff. Its mission is to guarantee the continuous functioning of the electricity system, while ensuring infrastructure is compatible with the protection of natural capital and species conservation.

title: DS3 Advisory Council
desc: Eirgrid‘s DS3 Advisory Council was established in 2011 to provide a forum to discuss the views and concerns of the DS3 Programme’s wide range of stakeholders on issues which impact on the successful implementation of the programme (DS3 = “Delivering a Secure, Sustainable Electricity System”).2014 Objectives 01 Discuss, review and ultimately help facilitate the progress of the […]

title: East Iceland Clean Energy Connections
desc: Landsnet applied load control schemes to the existing grid infrastructure in order to connect fish factories in East Iceland to the main grid using synchronised real-time measurements. This connection enabled the factories to replace crude oil for their energy consumption needs with renewable-sourced electricity from the main grid.
highlights: Transmission network constraint for eastward power flow raised by 44% when the control scheme is active; Societal benefits (90,000 tonnes of CO2 avoided) and environmental savings (13.4m €/year for 2 weeks of active control scheme); More flexible use of generation resources; Reduced customer outages after large disturbances; Provision of fast response capabilities by relevant electricity stakeholders
about: Rising environmental awareness and higher energy costs motivated fish factories in East Iceland to explore alternatives to crude oil. The transmission network was already constrained, and building new lines through remote, environmentally sensitive areas would have required significant investment. Landsnet applied new methods to the existing grid using fast synchronised wide area measurements and automated resource distribution from the transmission control centre. Factories could be tripped rapidly when needed to protect the wider grid. These measures increased usable capacity of the existing grid, allowing the fish industry to replace crude oil with renewable electricity from Iceland’s main grid. The practice improves grid reliability, flexibility, and environmental sustainability, demonstrating how innovative load control can maximise existing infrastructure. Landsnet, Iceland’s TSO, developed the practice in collaboration with fisheries, the DSO Rarik, a vendor (GE) providing design expertise and equipment, and a telecommunications provider (Orkufjarskipti) with high bandwidth optical fibre network. The approach is now part of the EU Horizon2020 MIGRATE project for further pilot testing and wider application across Iceland and potentially beyond.

title: Empowerment of citizens via crowd funding
desc: RTE uses crowdfunding to enhance public acceptability of new power lines. Through the My territorial projects platform, local initiatives receive co-funding from citizens, RTE, and the PAP (Plan d’Accompagnement de Projet), fostering sustainable development in communities affected by grid projects.
highlights: Crowdfunding platform launched on ULULE in 2016; 15 local projects pre-selected after a 2015 call; 2 projects successfully financed and implemented; PAP funds co-finance citizen-backed initiatives; RTE presented as a territorial development actor.
about: In January 2016, RTE launched the webpage My territorial projects on ULULE, a leading crowdfunding platform. Through this page, citizens can pitch entrepreneurial, cultural, agricultural, or other local projects. Once the crowdfunding goal is reached, RTE complements the financing through PAP funds, as defined in its public service contract with the French government. The PAP requires RTE to support sustainable economic development in areas affected by the construction of high-voltage transmission lines. By combining PAP financing with crowdfunding, RTE not only honours its contractual obligation but also engages citizens directly in project selection and funding. This participatory model allows residents to propose or support projects they consider valuable for their communities, while increasing transparency and visibility of PAP-supported initiatives. To guarantee feasibility, projects presented on ULULE were pre-selected by RTE and its partner ULULE, which also helped RTE design this participatory financing solution. In 2015, a call for projects was launched, resulting in 15 selected initiatives. Within just two and a half months, two of these projects were successfully financed and implemented through the crowdfunding mechanism. RTE, France’s electricity transmission system operator, is responsible for the operation, maintenance, and development of the high and extra-high voltage network. Its partner ULULE, specialised in crowdfunding, had successfully financed 13,106 projects across 179 countries between 2010 and 2015, with a 66% success rate and over 1.1 million members.

title: EnergizAIR
desc: EnergizAIR is an international initiative providing easy-to-understand indicators that show the current capacity for renewable energy production across Europe.
highlights: Adds renewable energy indicators to regular weather forecasts to promote home-grown energy use.; Covers 10 countries and follows a “franchise” model for easy expansion.; Indicators displayed via “solar PV”, “solar thermal” and “wind” icons showing the percentage of household needs met renewably.; Reached 6 million citizens via newspapers, radio, TV, and over 200 websites.; 80 weather team members trained as ambassadors for the project.; Survey shows 50% of recipients reported increased trust in renewables, 45% felt encouraged to invest.
about: EnergizAIR was created to bring renewable energy production information from the engineering sphere directly to consumers. By combining country-specific data such as energy consumption and renewable production, the project produces indicators showing what percentage of average household energy needs can be met by renewable sources in real time. These are represented graphically with “solar PV”, “solar thermal”, and “wind” icons, alongside percentages for potential electricity or hot water provision. The tool, embedded on more than 200 websites and adopted by multiple European media outlets, reaches millions with daily renewable energy potential information. It aims to emphasise the link between everyday weather conditions and renewable generation capacity, encouraging people to adopt home-grown energy both for personal use and as a contribution to grid management. Run by the Association for the Promotion of Renewable Energy (APERe) in Belgium, the project also trains weather presenters to interpret and explain the indicators, turning them into advocates for renewable energy awareness. APERe is an independent organisation dedicated to promoting renewable sources within the framework of rational energy use, leading educational initiatives, field activities, and fostering collaboration between academics and associations.

title: Green Corridors – Restoration of wildlife corridors under overhead lines in Belgium and France
desc: LIFE Elia-RTE was a 2011–2017 project combining grid safety with biodiversity-friendly vegetation management under high-voltage lines in Belgium and France. By creating green corridors through a multi-partner approach, it enhanced biodiversity, improved public perception, and proved more cost-effective than conventional vegetation management.
highlights: 1.4 to 3.9 times cheaper over 30 years than traditional clear-cutting.;70% of restoration sites rated successful; 82% had good or exceptional biological gains ;Contributed up to 10% of certain Natura 2000 habitat types in Belgium.
about: LIFE Elia-RTE, led by Belgian TSO Elia, French TSO RTE, and ecological consultancy Ecofirst, introduced a new approach to vegetation management beneath grid lines. Instead of traditional clear-cutting, the team restored forest edges and habitats, dug ponds, planted orchards, sowed meadows, and created pasture areas for local farmers. This biodiversity-friendly approach proved both ecologically beneficial and significantly more cost-efficient over 30 years. The project restored 20 hectares of natural habitats and improved biodiversity connectivity between conservation areas. By involving TSOs, authorities, forest owners, NGOs, academics, and local stakeholders, the team also improved acceptance for high-voltage lines. The 2023 “10 years-after report” analysed biological monitoring results: 70% of LIFE sites were deemed successful, with only one small site failing; 82% showed good or exceptional biological gains. Many restored areas are classified as Annex I habitats under the EU Habitats Directive, where conservation status is otherwise poor. Notably, 111 ha out of 136 ha of grasslands and heathlands in Elia corridors are recognised as Natura 2000 habitats, representing 1–10% of Belgium’s total surface of their type. The €3 million project was co-financed by the European Commission (38%), Walloon region (27%), Elia (22%), and RTE (13%). After 2017, Elia continued the work through its “Ecological Corridors” programme, expanding practices across its grid. The approach has since been shared with TSOs from 17 EU member states, influencing biodiversity strategies across Europe.

title: HV Voltage Source Converter for Skagerrak 4 Interconnector
desc: Skagerrak 4 is a subsea interconnector between Norway and Denmark using Voltage Source Converter (VSC) technology to strengthen grid stability, enable black-start capability, reduce harmonic distortion, and integrate wind and hydro generation while enhancing electricity supply security.
highlights: Increased capacity from 1,000 MW to 1,700 MW, combining wind and hydropower systems for optimal balance.; Multilevel VSC offers independent active/reactive power control—no capacitor banks needed.:Enables black-start capability, improved voltage control, and reduced filters and commutation failures.
about: For the Skagerrak 4 subsea interconnector between Norway and Denmark, Statnett deployed Voltage Source Converter (VSC) technology to secure electricity supply while combining wind and hydro energy. The new technology enhances AC grid stability, lowers filter requirements, and allows black-start of blackout grids. Skagerrak 4 expanded interconnector capacity from 1,000 to 1,700 MW, allowing complementary operation between Denmark’s intermittent wind and slow thermal plants and Norway’s fast, controllable hydropower. The multilevel VSC consists of transistors and diodes in parallel with capacitors, which are switched to build a controlled AC source with low harmonic distortion and stable DC voltage. Unlike traditional HVDC systems—where reactive consumption rises with transmitted power, the converter independently controls active and reactive power, eliminating the need for capacitor banks. Statnett, Norway’s state-owned system operator, runs 11,000 km of high-voltage lines, 150 power stations, and cross-border links. Skagerrak 4 is co-owned by Energinet.dk and Statnett, and the technology development was carried out in collaboration with ABB.

title: Joint public power line planning
topic: Public acceptance and engagement, Energy system planning
desc: TenneT, in cooperation with KWI (Institute for Advanced Study in the Humanities, Essen), developed and implemented a new set of participation concepts to enhance early citizen involvement in grid expansion planning. The approach aimed to identify route corridors together with citizens, ensure transparency and accountability, and integrate local knowledge into the official planning process.
highlights: Early-stage citizen engagement to develop route alternatives.; Focus on transparency, comprehensibility, and accountability.; Tested along the Redwitz–Schwandorf line in two challenging planning areas.; Combination of public events, workshops, continuous planning groups, and info-marts.; Introduction of “Route Assessment Teams” — mixed groups of citizens, NGOs, agencies, and TenneT staff.; Supported by the German government-funded Demoenergy project.
about: Traditionally, participation processes in grid planning did not allow citizens to influence routing decisions. With research and co-financing from the Demoenergy project, TenneT and KWI created new participation formats, assisted by external consultants and facilitators. The concept was tested in two planning areas with particular challenges along the Redwitz–Schwandorf line. Each location hosted three public events, supported by TenneT’s established info-mart format, which facilitates informal, one-on-one exchanges between citizens and project staff. A central feature was the “Route Assessment Teams,” made up of randomly selected citizens, NGO representatives, municipal and forestry agencies, and TenneT staff. These teams held in-depth discussions on routing options, providing TenneT with insights into local sensitivities and preferences. The process built trust by ensuring transparency and giving stakeholders a tangible influence on route development. While info-marts had been used in previous high-voltage projects, this participatory approach expanded beyond the conventional format, integrating collaborative workshops and continuous planning groups to improve inclusivity and knowledge-sharing.

title: Man-made nest programme
desc: MAVIR installed and monitored man-made nests on power pylons to protect the endangered Saker Falcon, reducing mortality and power interruptions.
highlights: Over 250 Saker Falcons now nest on MAVIR pylons, representing about 75% of Hungary’s population.; In 2014, 1.2 million viewers from 100 countries followed live nest camera footage.; MAVIR receives 3–5 monthly requests from institutions to present its bird protection activities.
about: Facing bird mortality and related power interruptions, MAVIR identified affected bird populations and areas, launching a man-made nest programme adapted to birds’ physiological needs and the transmission network’s characteristics. In collaboration with national parks and NGOs, secure breeding habitats were provided, particularly to halt the decline of the Saker Falcon, an endangered species nesting on pylons. Studies revealed that more than 250 Saker Falcons now nest on MAVIR pylons, about 75% of the national population. The initiative includes continuous online nest monitoring and public engagement through events for children and adults. In 2014, 1.2 million people from 100 countries followed a live nest camera. MAVIR’s bird conservation blog, launched in 2014, attracts thousands of readers. The organisation receives regular invitations to present its work, with feedback showing increased public awareness about bird life. MAVIR, owner and operator of Hungary’s transmission network, also manages areas within the Natura 2000 network, the EU’s flagship biodiversity conservation programme aimed at safeguarding Europe’s most threatened species and habitats.

title: Minimise cable impact on marine ecosystem
desc: Terna developed an innovative methodology for laying submarine cables that minimizes environmental impacts and protects sensitive habitats such as the endangered seagrass Posidonia oceanica. Applied to the Malta–Sicily interconnector, this practice ensures sustainable construction while supporting renewable energy integration in Malta’s electricity system.
highlights: Replaced bentonite drilling fluid with biodegradable Xantan gum; Transplanted Posidonia oceanica meadows to a safe site in Sicily; First-year monitoring confirmed good condition of original and transplanted seagrass.
about: To enhance Malta’s energy security and enable greater renewable integration, a submarine interconnector between Malta and Sicily was planned by Terna. The route crossed areas rich in Posidonia oceanica, a threatened seagrass vital for marine biodiversity. Conventional drilling would have used bentonite, risking suffocation of the meadows through sediment deposition. Instead, Terna introduced a portfolio of innovative techniques, including the use of Xantan gum, a biodegradable polysaccharide, as a safe drilling lubricant. The drilling path and methods were further adapted to minimize seabed disturbance, while unavoidable impacts were offset through transplantation of affected seagrass rhizomes to a monitored site in Sicily. After one year of environmental monitoring, both the original and transplanted meadows showed healthy conditions, demonstrating the success of the methodology. The project was developed collaboratively with the Italian Ministry of Environment, the Environmental Protection Agency, and Nexans. The Malta–Sicily interconnector, owned by ENEMALTA and part of the Trans-European Energy Network, was co-financed through the European Energy Program for Recovery. Terna, which manages over 72,000 km of high-voltage lines in Italy with 3,700 employees, has established itself as a European leader in environmentally conscious grid expansion practices.

title: Net demand ramping variability
desc: The California Independent System Operator (CAISO) introduced a real-time measurement system for net demand ramping variability, improving grid reliability amid growing renewable energy integration. This approach allows better planning, faster response, and compliance with operating standards.
highlights: Net demand ramping variability is now tracked minute-by-minute for improved forecasting; The new method replaced monthly performance evaluations; CAISO manages 80% of California’s high-voltage grid infrastructure.
about: To handle the volatility of renewables, CAISO developed a granular approach to monitor “net-load”—the system demand minus renewable generation—on a minute-by-minute basis. This helps the grid operator anticipate when conventional power plants need to ramp up to compensate for drops in renewable production, especially during the evening hours. By calculating net demand ramping variability over a three-hour period, CAISO ensures there is enough ramping capability to maintain grid balance, frequency, and reliability. This level of precision allows for better planning decisions and regulatory recommendations to the California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC). Previously, CAISO used a monthly performance average over a 12-month period. The new system, which evaluates performance every minute and averages it across an hour, gives a clearer picture of actual operating conditions. This finer resolution also exposed data gaps, leading to improved internal systems to accommodate higher renewable penetration. As the independent grid operator for most of California and parts of Nevada, CAISO manages 26,000 circuit miles of high-voltage power lines and plays a key role in integrating diverse energy sources into the market.

title: Preventing Electrocution of Endangered Birds
desc: Cooperation between NGOs, industry and governments to prevent electrocution of endangered birds on distribution power lines in Bulgaria and Sudan, led by the Bulgarian Society for the Protection of Birds (BSPB) and the BirdLife International – UNDP/GEF Migratory Soaring Birds Project. Objectives 01 Identifies power lines that are a threat to bird species. 02 Replaces […]

title: The future of energy Turning young people into aware citizens
desc: In cooperation with its nature conservation partners, 50Hertz is implementing a range of both fun and informative communication activities that specifically target young adolescents and the communities in which they live. Objectives 01 Convey the challenges of the energy transition and of grid development to young people 02 Establish a strong network within the affected […]

title: The future of energy – turning young people into aware citizens
desc: In cooperation with nature conservation partners, 50Hertz carries out a range of fun and informative communication activities aimed at young adolescents and their communities.
highlights: Targets youth awareness on the challenges of Germany’s energy transition and grid development.; Jointly developed a nature trail with a local school, including nesting aids, lizard habitats, and a beehive.; Rehabilitated an orchard with 200 trees as a learning environment for schools.; Created the “Mitmachausstellung” participatory exhibition with UfU, combining theory and hands-on experience.; Over 3,000 school children and 800 adults have participated.; Activities are designed for repeatability and long-term impact.
about: The initiative was inspired by a lack of awareness among young people about the challenges of Germany’s energy transition and related grid projects, which often contributes to low community acceptance. To address this, 50Hertz launched a set of joint solution-finding and educational activities with local stakeholders to increase knowledge of environmental issues and grid development. A key element was the creation of a nature trail in cooperation with students from a local school near a proposed power line route. The trail features nesting aids for bats and birds, habitats for lizards, and a beehive. This was complemented by the rehabilitation of an orchard, integrating 200 trees into a renewed learning space where schools can engage with orchard environments and fruit pressing. Together with the Independent Institute for Environmental Issues (UfU), 50Hertz developed the “Mitmachausstellung” participatory exhibition, blending theoretical learning with practical, playful experiences to raise awareness of the energy transition. The project engaged municipalities, authorities, schools, teachers, pupils, parents, NGOs, and other local actors. All activities are built to be repeatable and to generate lasting positive effects for the community. 50Hertz operates, maintains, plans, and expands the 380/220 kV transmission grid across northern and eastern Germany. Over 42% of consumption in its grid area is already supplied by renewable sources, making it a global leader in this field.

title: The Variable Ratio Distribution Transformer (VRDT)
desc: The Variable Ratio Distribution Transformer (VRDT) is a simple, cost-efficient alternative to conventional low-voltage grid expansion. By decoupling low voltage from medium voltage, it releases unused capacity in the medium voltage grid, enabling better integration of renewable energy without major infrastructure expansion.
highlights: Total planned VRDT-related expansion costs: €2.3 million; 114 VRDT units already in operation by Avacon AG. ; Improves voltage quality while avoiding costly parallel cable laying.
about: With the increasing share of renewable energy as a distributed source, Germany’s electricity network requires adaptation. Low- and medium-voltage grids, which already host most renewable installations, must adjust to the changing production-consumption balance. Avacon AG’s VRDT technology converts a medium voltage bandwidth into a regulated reference voltage at the low-voltage bus bar of a local secondary substation. This decouples low voltage from medium voltage, making the entire ±10% nominal voltage bandwidth available separately for each. This frees up capacity in the medium voltage grid that was previously reserved for the low voltage, improving voltage quality and enabling cost-effective renewable integration. Compared to traditional grid expansion measures, such as laying parallel cables or replacing transformers, VRDT offers significant savings and efficiency gains. Scenario comparisons show VRDT as the preferable economic option when integrating additional renewable generation. Avacon AG is the European leader in VRDT implementation, with 114 units currently in operation. The company operates power and gas networks and provides infrastructure services across large parts of western and central Germany, supplying energy to 16 million people and employing around 1,700 staff in 15 locations.

title: Transparent transmission grid planning
topic: Advocating for optimised grids, Energy system planning
desc: Germanwatch, a German climate and environment organization, closely monitored the implementation of new legislation on transmission grid planning and actively participated in expert discussions. Their focus was on ensuring that grid planning aligns with ambitious climate and energy targets, is transparent and participative, and adheres to high environmental standards.
highlights: Engagement in expert workshops, position papers, and policy discussions.; Advocacy for ambitious climate and energy targets in grid planning.; Publication of a handbook for local stakeholders to participate in planning processes.; Support for applying the “European Grid Declaration on Electricity Network Development and Nature Conservation” in national and European projects.; Public support, alongside other NGOs, for north–south transmission lines crucial to Germany’s energy transition.
about: In 2011, Germany introduced a new transmission grid planning procedure, offering more opportunities for public participation. Based on a scenario framework for future power generation and consumption, German transmission system operators (TSOs) draft a ten-year grid development plan each year, which is then approved by the central permitting and regulatory agency. Germanwatch contributed to this process through active participation in expert workshops, drafting position papers, and producing a handbook to guide local stakeholders in engaging with planning procedures. They maintained ongoing dialogue with European, national, and local stakeholders to develop recommendations for grid operators and policymakers. The organization also played an active advocacy role, collaborating with other NGOs such as NABU, WWF, and DUH to issue public statements supporting power lines deemed essential for transforming the electricity system. One notable effort was their joint press release endorsing north–south transmission lines in Germany. Germanwatch e.V. is an independent development and environmental organization committed to promoting North–South equity and safeguarding livelihoods. Its work focuses on the energy and climate politics of the Global North and their worldwide impacts.

title: Équilibre Pylon design
topic: Public acceptance and engagement, Circularity and supply chains
desc: The Équilibre Pylon is an innovative 400 kV pylon design created for the replacement of an existing line. Developed by RTE with public involvement, it combines aesthetic integration into the landscape with technical improvements, enhancing social acceptance and functionality.
highlights: Develop an aesthetic design for pylons; Work on the successful integration of pylons into the landscape; Improve several technical aspects of the pylon structure; Create public acceptance regarding the overhead line replacement project
about: To improve the visual integration and public acceptance of an upgraded overhead line, RTE engaged 1.7 million people affected by the project. The public was invited to choose a power line concept suited to local constraints. Following a tender that attracted 80 proposals from designers and architects, RTE’s experts shortlisted three concepts. These were discussed in expert commissions with citizen input, resulting in the selection of the Équilibre pylon designed by Hugh Dutton. Shaped like a balanced arrow pointing skyward, the name “Équilibre” (French for “Balance”) reflects both stability and harmony with nature. Standing 70 metres tall, the pylon is made of rolled steel plate, comparable in form to wind turbines rather than traditional lattice towers. The 30 km 400 kV line, 16 km of which will feature Équilibre pylons, began construction in November 2019, with commissioning planned for Autumn 2021. The project cost is estimated at €1.88 million per km, totalling €56.4 million. The pylon’s triangle cable configuration reduces the distance between phases in each circuit, lowering the strength of the magnetic field. RTE, the French electricity transmission network operator, is responsible for operating, maintaining, and developing the high and extra-high voltage network while ensuring its security and reliability.

title: CHP Accumulator System
desc: The practice used a water-based system as a heat accumulator – and essentially, also an electricity accumulator – in order to respond to heat consumption peaks and increase the general efficiency of the region’s combined heat and power (CHP) plant. The heat accumulator used in the project is a steel tank containing 2.400 m3 of water. Objectives […]

title: Community Dialogue for Suedlink
desc: TenneT’s “Community dialogue for SuedLink” is a communication strategy encompassing local participation in the form of info-marts that allow for on-the-ground dialogue and involve citizens in the planning of SuedLink. Objectives 01 Build an “equal-to-equal” communication strategy in order to manage the high levels of opposition to the project. 02 Increase acceptability among local people. […]

title: DER Integration System
desc: The “DER Integration system” is a system developed by the Italian branch of the ENEL Group, that, combining various technological measures, increases hosting capacity for distributed generation and ensures operation security, allowing for voltage and power flow control in a smart grid architecture. objectives 01 Solve the main problems arising in active grids including hosting capacity, […]

title: Eagle Pylon
desc: Bystrup developed the Eagle Pylon, an innovative design for electricity pylons, providing an alternative to classic lattice towers. It addresses visual amenity concerns, is easier to produce, transport, install, and maintain, and creates a positive image while ensuring efficiency in transmission.
highlights: Gain public acceptability and positively influence the image of pylons; Create a pylon that addresses visual amenity concerns, is easy to produce, transport, install and maintain ;Create a family of pylons with an identical general appearance that build an unbroken structure in the landscape
about: The Eagle Pylon is typically 35 metres tall – about 15 metres shorter than the equivalent 400 kV lattice tower – and designed as a shaft with arms reaching towards the sky. The form represents optimism and sends a positive signal to its surroundings. The shaft and cross-arms are made of hot-dip galvanised steel, with stainless steel wires holding the arms in place. Created as more than just a visual alternative, it improves on lattice towers in all aspects: gaining public acceptability, creating a positive image, simplifying production and installation, and reducing maintenance. The design uses as few elements as possible, has a simple monopile foundation, and can be built by one team of five people in one day. The Eagle Pylon family includes suspension, flying angle, tension, and dead-end tension types, all with the same general appearance for a seamless line in the landscape. Due to strong public opposition to lattice towers, it replaced old pylons on a 180 km Danish transmission line connecting Germany and Norway. Completed in 2014, this “Eagle Line” is now the backbone of Denmark’s transmission system. Bystrup, a Danish company of designers and engineers, worked with Energinet.dk to develop the Eagle Pylon after public preference shifted from the proposed Sky Pylon.

title: Experience Orchard
desc: Development of an orchard area as part of the mitigation and compensation measures portfolio of 50Herts that combined both environmental and social aspects. objectives 01 Integrate social activities and cooperation on a regular basis into the compensatory measures process 02 Utilize the orchard area with a larger social impact and a larger participation of stakeholders […]

title: New power grids and nature conservation
desc: NABU’s power grids and nature conservation project promotes an energy transition sensitive to environmental concerns by enhancing the involvement of conservation organisations in grid projects through diverse engagement and communication efforts.
highlights: NABU positions itself as a constructive partner for Germany’s Energiewende.;Activities include guidance papers, workshops, seminars with field trips, and factsheets.;Over 560,000 members support NABU, one of Germany’s oldest and largest nature associations.;Topics covered range from bird protection on lines to biotope networks and offshore wind grid planning.
about: To ensure genuinely nature-friendly grid development, NABU promotes integrated communication between authorities, conservationists, and the energy industry. The project operates under the belief that involving conservationists early in discussions prevents delays or lawsuits and fosters collaborative decision-making. The initiative comprises various communication activities: producing guidance papers on conservation and grids, organising collaborative workshops and information seminars with field trips, and publishing factsheets. Participants include environmental associations, TSOs, planning authorities, scientists, local politicians, and the general public.
