These sessions received very positive feedback, and TSOs acknowledged the value of integrating insights into more thorough, prudent, and transparent grid planning. Workshops, seminars, and publications covered key topics such as bird protection on overhead lines; water and soil conservation with underground cables; grid connections for offshore wind farms; landscape and habitat fragmentation; ecological line management; biotope networks; and grid planning across Northern and Southern Germany. Founded in 1899, NABU is one of Germany’s oldest and largest environmental associations, with over 560,000 members committed to conserving habitats, species, and climate protection. NABU also serves as Germany’s BirdLife Partner.

title: Pulse Heating
desc: ESCSH developed a district heating system using both pulse heat and solar panels in order to reduce heat losses in supply pipes and supply low-energy houses in an energy efficient way.
highlights: Reduce heat losses in heating supply pipes ;Provide higher supply temperatures to houses; Evaluate and monitor installation capacity through data measurement
about: ESCSH equipped 20 low-energy houses in the Cooperative Society Hjortshøj near Aarhus with a solar collector and a storage tank. Each house was also connected to the local district heating supply. In regular district heating the heat flow in form of hot water or steam is continuous and a considerable amount of heat gets lost when consumption is low (and due to the slower flow, the water cools before it reaches the consumer). In order to attenuate the energy loss, a so-called pulse heating was applied to the 20 houses: hot district heating water is sent in pulses. For a 3-4 months summer period no heat is sent via the district system, but necessary heat is provided by local solar heating. The heating pulses are regulated by a timer that is set to four hours a day for the warmer periods, increased to twelve hours a day for colder periods and providing a permanent heat supply for the coldest winter periods. A second regulation stops heat supply to the houses when the tank is warm – based on the thermostats in each house's storage tank. The same heat storage tanks are used to store both solar heating and heat from the district heating in order to ensure heating between the heat pulses. Thanks to the pulse heating technique, total savings amounted to about 50% of heat losses in the local heat supply network. The system also allows heat supply to be scheduled for the hours of the day, where heat is cheapest or most environmental, for instance from a large heat pump driven by wind power. Additional information: Energiselskabet ved Andelssamfundet i Hjortshøj is the Energy Supply Organisation of the Cooperative Society in Hjortshøj, Denmark. The Cooperative Community of this Ecovillage, about 15 km northwest of Aarhus, is an intentional community started in 1991. The community combines environmental buildings, environmental energy supply, car sharing, and organic farming, with a common goal to live a more environmentally friendly lifestyle The pulse heating project also involved an engineering consultant, Plan-energi, and Aarhus Municipality as an associate partner. The project is sup-ported by the Danish District Heating Association

title: Stork Platform Campaign
desc: EVN Bulgaria’s Stork Platform Campaign installs artificial nesting platforms on distribution power poles to protect white storks from electrocution and improve grid reliability. The platforms maintain safe distances between nests and electric parts, reducing bird mortality while safeguarding infrastructure.
highlights: 1651 nesting platforms installed since the campaign began.; 61% of platforms are regularly used by birds.; Installation periods aligned with migration and breeding seasons.;Reduced bird mortality and improved grid reliability.;Strong cooperation with NGOs, citizens, and local authorities.
about: EVN Bulgaria Elektrorazpredelenie EAD operates in South-East Bulgaria, supplying electricity to more than 1.5 million customers. Although installing nesting platforms is not part of its core business, it is an established practice within its environmental policy. Bulgaria has one of the largest migration flows of the white stork, a protected species under the national Biodiversity Act. As old trees have disappeared, storks have increasingly built nests on power poles, risking electrocution and grid damage. The platforms prevent direct contact between nests and power lines, avoiding short circuits and sparking. The campaign has significantly reduced stork mortality, improved infrastructure safety, and fostered cooperation with environmental organisations such as the Bulgarian Society for Protection of Birds, Green Balkan, and Foundation Biodiversity, as well as active participation from citizens in reporting stork-related incidents.

desc: The sectoral plan for transmission lines is the overriding planning instrument for new power lines in Switzerland. During this procedure, possible conflicts are identified, solutions developed and in the end the best possible corridor for the new line is determined. Throughout the process, an advisory group is regularly consulted.During discussions, project specific knowledge is exchanged and possible areas of conflicts are identified. Together with the group, a scoping document for the Environmental Impact Assessment, which is conducted during the next stage, the plan approval procedure, is compiled.

title: Citizens Transmission
desc: The Citizens Transmission project, created by Joseph P. Kennedy II, dedicates 50% of profits from high-voltage transmission lines to charitable energy assistance programmes for low-income households. By combining infrastructure investment with social impact, the initiative delivers renewable energy while supporting vulnerable communities.
highlights: 50% of transmission line profits fund charitable energy programmes; USD 100 million invested in Sunrise Powerlink with 50% capacity rights; Over 500 free solar rooftops installed for low-income households; Each system cuts household electricity costs by up to 50%; 20-year prepaid lease guarantees no financial obligations for homeowners.
about: In 2005, Citizens partnered with San Diego Gas & Electric (SDG&E) to develop the Sunrise Powerlink, a USD 1.8 billion, 120-mile, 500 kV transmission line connecting San Diego County to renewable energy from Imperial County. The project aimed to carry green power from wind, solar, and geothermal resources while using profits to fund clean energy benefits for low-income families in one of California’s poorest regions, where temperatures often reach 48 degrees Celsius. Citizens invested USD 100 million for rights to 50% of the transmission capacity in Imperial County. This capacity was transferred to the regional grid operator, allowing Citizens to earn a federally regulated rate-of-return. Since the line became operational in 2012, Citizens has provided more than 500 free residential solar rooftop installations through the Citizens Solar Homes Programme. These systems reduce electricity costs by up to 50% annually for each household, freeing up income for essential needs such as food and medicine. The programme uses a 20-year pre-paid lease, ensuring no maintenance or equipment replacement costs for homeowners. Founded in 1979 by Joseph P. Kennedy II, Citizens has consistently reinvested commercial revenues into charitable ventures in the U.S. and abroad. It has supported heating assistance, reduced prescription drug costs, and implemented solar heating projects in the Caribbean basin. Currently, Citizens is partnering on two additional transmission projects in California, as well as projects in New York and Vermont, with profits earmarked for further charitable energy initiatives.

title: Evaluation scheme for underground cables vs. overhead lines
desc: Whether transmission lines are to be constructed overhead or placed underground in Switzerland is being decided on a case-by-case basis and in accordance with objective criteria. For this purpose, the Swiss Federal Office of Energy (SFOE) has developed a "transmission lines evaluation model”.
highlights: The main objective of the new model is to evaluate various options in order to define the most suitable corridor for transmission lines. At the same time, the question whether transmission lines should be placed overhead or underground can be answered and the licensing procedure can therefore be accelerated. The model comprises four pillars, each of which contains three or four groups of criteria (spatial development, technology, environment and the economy). Projects are examined and evaluated on the basis of the criteria. After initial findings have been obtained, minor adjustments will be made to the new model and a manual will then be prepared. The application of this evaluation model to every new project is mandatory for Swissgrid. This model makes decisions more transparent and helps stakeholders understand which criteria were taken into account when coming up with the decision.

title: BNetzA Meets Science
desc: “BNetzA meets science” is a two-day interdisciplinary dialogue and networking event fostering collaboration, knowledge transfer, and intergenerational exchange between stakeholders engaged in the research and development of current and future grids.
highlights: Shed light on the intricacies of grid planning ;Enrich the public participation process though interdisciplinary exchange ;provide formal and informal exchanges, help share knowledge and develop networks
about: “BNetzA meets science” is an open, interdisciplinary dialogue and networking event with an intergenerational component, connecting students, researchers, professors, think tanks, NGOs, and other stakeholders in the energy sector. It aims to shed light on the complexities of grid planning, enrich public participation through exchange, and foster knowledge sharing. Core thematic areas include Technology & Engineering, Communication Science, Economics, and Landscape. Topics are explored through informal discussions, brainstorming sessions, and subject-specific workshops. The event concludes with a panel discussion, and those unable to attend in person can follow proceedings live via Twitter. All outcomes are documented on the BNetzA website and in a dedicated proceedings document. Feedback highlights the event’s value: 95% of participants would recommend it, and 95.5% reported gaining new insights on the discussed issues. The “Federal Network Agency” (BNetzA) for Electricity, Gas, Telecommunications, Post and Railway promotes effective competition, ensures non-discriminatory access to networks, and regulates Germany’s electricity and gas markets. Established in 1998 with headquarters in Bonn, the agency organises the event through the Participation Section of its Grid Expansion Department. BNetzA also collaborates with other European regulators, the European Commission, and various supranational institutions and organisations.
